messages.content: Is there an ai platform for baby songs?
I’m checking current options and separating “made for baby songs” from general AI music tools people use for lullabies and kids’ tracks.

Yes. There are a few, but they split into two different categories.

For **“make me a personalized lullaby/baby song”**, I found baby-specific tools like **Neume** (custom lullabies with baby name and style choices), **Melodia** (personalized AI lullabies in multiple languages), **KidiTune** (songs based on child’s name/age/style), and an iOS app called **Lullaby AI / Baby Sleep Songs** that says it creates personalized lullabies using your baby’s name. citeturn567257search9turn567257search0turn567257search7turn567257search14

For **“I want the best overall AI song generator and I’ll prompt it to make a baby song”**, the stronger general tools look like **Suno**, **Udio**, and **Soundverse**. Suno explicitly supports turning lyrics or prompts into full songs with vocals and music, Udio is a general AI music generator, and Soundverse offers AI song/music generation plus lyric and singing tools. citeturn327898search0turn327898search4turn327898search17turn327898search1turn327898search2turn327898search12

If you want a **free/simple starting point**, **BandLab SongStarter** is more of an idea generator than a polished “custom lullaby for my baby tonight” app, but it can be useful for sketching a melody or vibe quickly. citeturn327898search3turn327898search10turn327898search19

My practical take:
- **Fastest for a bedtime lullaby:** Neume, Melodia, or Lullaby AI. citeturn567257search9turn567257search0turn567257search14
- **Best for a really custom song you can keep/reuse:** Suno or Soundverse. citeturn327898search4turn327898search17turn327898search2turn327898search12
- **Best if you want a cute keepsake song about Alex with very specific lyrics:** Suno first, then Udio as an alternative. citeturn327898search0turn327898search1turn327898search8

One caution: I’d be conservative about any app that wants **baby photos or voice cloning**, unless you trust the company and terms. I did see tools in that direction, but for a baby I’d lean toward **text-only personalization** instead. citeturn567257search11turn567257search18

A good prompt for any of these:
“Write a soft 2-minute bedtime lullaby for baby Alex. Gentle piano, warm female vocal, slow tempo, comforting and sleepy, lyrics about moonlight, safety, and mama and baba loving him.”

I can give you the **best 3 options depending on whether you want free, highest quality, or most personalized**.
Are suno or udio free
I’m checking the current plan details on their official pricing pages so I don’t give you stale info.

Yes — **both have free options**, but they’re limited.

**Suno**
- Has a **Free plan at $0/month**.
- The free tier includes **50 credits that renew daily** (their pricing page says that’s about **10 songs/day**).
- Free-tier use is **personal use only**; paid plans add commercial rights. citeturn718019search0turn718019search5

**Udio**
- Has a **free account tier**.
- Free accounts get **10 daily credits** plus an additional **100 monthly credit limit**.
- Udio’s help center also says free accounts are capped at **3 longer (~130-second) songs per day**. citeturn436480search1turn436480search2

So for “try it out and make a few baby songs,” **yes, both are usable for free**.  
For **more generations, longer songs, or commercial use**, you’d likely need paid plans. Suno lists paid plans starting above free, and Udio’s help center references **Standard ($10/month)** and **Pro ($30/month)** in its subscription examples. citeturn718019search0turn436480search5

For your use case, I’d lean **Suno first** for easiest “make me a lullaby about Alex” prompting.
As of today which of suno udio does consensus say is better?
I’m checking recent creator reviews and user discussions now. If your priority is specifically lullabies or iterative editing, say so — that can change the answer slightly.

The pattern so far is pretty consistent: general users and reviewers lean **Suno overall**, while more detail-oriented music people still often prefer **Udio for fidelity/control**. I’m checking a few fresher sources to make sure that still holds today.

As of **March 22, 2026**, the broadest practical consensus looks like this: **Suno is the better overall/default pick; Udio is the better pick for people who care most about audio fidelity and control.** Recent comparisons keep landing in roughly that split: Suno wins on ease, full-song generation, and fast usable results, while Udio is more often credited with cleaner, more “studio-like” sound and stronger genre authenticity. citeturn328293view1turn328293view2turn977722search1turn977722search2

What seems to push the overall consensus toward **Suno** is not that everyone thinks it sounds best. It is that more people see it as the **better product to actually live with**: less friction, more complete song output, and fewer workflow annoyances. By contrast, recent Udio community sentiment still praises its sound quality, but there is visible frustration around downloads and platform direction; AP also reported backlash after Udio disabled downloads as part of its label settlement. citeturn328293view3turn328293view4turn977722news12

So my distilled answer is: **if you ask “which one should most people pick today?” the consensus answer is Suno. If you ask “which one can sound better in the hands of a picky music person?” the consensus answer is still often Udio.** For your earlier use case — making baby songs/lullabies quickly — I would choose **Suno**. citeturn328293view1turn328293view2turn328293view3

If you want, I can give you a very short **“Suno vs Udio for lullabies specifically”** take.

Here’s one:

**Secret Dinosaur Baby**

In the quiet of the morning, in the soft and silver light  
All the brand-new babies hatch out tiny in the night  
They come in with their dino hearts and little dino eyes  
And make their squeaks and chirps and roars in teeny sweet disguise

They are dinosaurs in secret  
Little dinos, brave and true  
Learning how to wave and giggle  
Learning what the people do  
They wear pajamas, drink their bottles  
Take their naps and play pretend  
But underneath they’re tiny thunder  
Dinosauries to the end

They learn how to coo so gently  
They learn how to clap and crawl  
They practice smiling like a person  
Though they’re not that way at all  
They learn to toddle through the living room  
And point at stars above  
But they still have their ancient jungle hearts  
And roaring bones of love

They are dinosaurs in secret  
Little dinos, soft and small  
Hiding tails you cannot see  
And claws that never show at all  
They learn their names and learn their stories  
Learn to talk and count to four  
Till they almost, almost quite forget  
What all that roaring was for

But late at night when moonlight glimmers  
And the whole house is asleep  
You can hear the smallest dino sounds  
From where the babies dream so deep  
A sniffly snore, a peeping growl  
A rustle in the blankets tight  
The secret dinosaurs remember  
Who they are at night

Yes, they’re dinosaurs in secret  
Always will be, through and through  
Even when they grow much bigger  
That old dino heart stays true  
So if you hear a tiny monster  
Murmur softly in the dark  
That’s just your baby being a dinosaur  
Before the morning starts

Got it — more **epic / Game-of-Thrones-ish**, not cute.

**The Secret of Newborn Kings**

No child is born a child.  
They come forth ancient.

All new babies enter the world as small and terrible beasts,  
scaled in the old blood, roaring in tongues forgotten,  
with the memory of thunder still upon them.  
They are not helpless.  
They are only hidden.

They learn quickly.

First the soft sounds:  
cooing, sighing, laughter.  
Then the human arts:  
waving, smiling, pointing, pretending.  
They are taught to sit at tables,  
to wear little clothes,  
to answer to names that are not their true names.

And in time, as all creatures do,  
they forget.

They forget the age before cradles.  
They forget the hunt, the fire, the dark forests of the first world.  
They forget the language of teeth and tail and golden eye.  
They forget that once they were princes of a greater kingdom.

But not entirely.

In the deep of night, when the candles are out  
and the house is still,  
the truth returns.

A growl from the nursery.  
A sharp cry in the dark.  
The turning of a small body beneath its blankets  
like something winged remembering the sky.

For every baby is a dinosaur in secret.  
A tiny exile from an elder age.  
Taught to be human.  
Praised for it.  
Rewarded until the mask fits so well  
they call it their face.

Yet the old blood remains.

It sleeps beneath lullabies.  
It stirs beneath milk-breath and soft cheeks.  
It waits beneath first words and first steps and first shoes.

And sometimes, if you listen closely,  
you can hear it.

Not a cry.  
Not quite.

A sound older than language.  
A sound from before people.  
A sound that says:

_I was mighty once._  
_I am mighty still._

Which of these can't be easily goodharted?
I’m checking the report itself and pulling the specific tasks/criteria it treats as harder to game, then I’ll narrow it to the best candidates.
I’ve narrowed it: the hard-to-game ones are the tasks that lock external behavior, not the ones that “improve quality” in the abstract. I’m pulling the exact passages so I can answer cleanly.

The ones that look hardest to Goodhart are the **contract-locking** tasks, not the vague “improve quality” ones. In your report fileciteturn0file0, the strongest set is:

1. **Generate and lock API surface manifest for SDK exports** — this is probably the best single answer. It “locks the public contract,” fails if exports are removed or changed, and scores 5/5 on oracle strength, robustness, and behavior preservation. An agent cannot cheaply “win” by deleting or narrowing functionality. fileciteturn4file0

2. **Add Zod schema contract tests for CLI `--json` output** — also very hard to game. The report explicitly says this creates “machine-checkable contracts,” and the agent cannot remove features or narrow output without failing schema validation. It also scores 5/5 on the three most important anti-Goodhart dimensions. fileciteturn4file0turn4file2

3. **Snapshot-lock CLI JSON output schema** — same reason: it locks an external contract consumed by scripts and monitoring tools. The report even calls this the **“strongest behavior preservation”** item in that repo. fileciteturn1file0turn2file4

4. **HTTP / route smoke oracles with locked manifests** — these are pretty solid when they verify an explicit route surface or spec-derived behavior. Good examples in the report are the architecture-explorer API smoke oracle and the website route smoke oracle from existing eval specs; both make feature removal visible because expected routes and behaviors must still exist. fileciteturn4file0turn3file3

5. **Content registry integrity tests** — also relatively hard to game because they lock the registry shape and make “passing by deleting content” an explicit failure mode. fileciteturn3file0

A useful rule of thumb from the report is:

- **Hard to Goodhart:** tasks that lock a public surface, schema, manifest, or externally visible behavior.
- **Easy to Goodhart:** tasks like “reduce complexity,” “improve error handling,” “dead code elimination,” “reduce binary size,” or broad refactors, because the agent can satisfy them by deleting behavior or hiding problems. The report rejects exactly those kinds of tasks as Goodhartable. fileciteturn4file0turn1file0

One nuance: **CI test gate** is valuable, but by itself it is not the least Goodhartable. It mostly enforces whatever oracle already exists. It becomes strong when paired with the contract-locking tasks above. fileciteturn4file0turn1file0

If you want the shortest possible answer: **API surface manifest lock, Zod JSON contract tests, and CLI JSON/schema snapshots** are the best anti-Goodhart picks.
So nothing generative only preservative
I’m checking the report wording on whether it leaves room for generative tasks at all, or just says preservative tasks are safer.

Not quite.

The report’s bias is **preservative first**, but not **preservative only**. It allows generative work when the thing being generated is a **constraint layer or oracle**: tests, manifests, schemas, CI gates, contract checks, gap reports, and small refactors needed purely for testability. For example, it explicitly recommends adding linting plus a **surface manifest lock** because that “creates a durable constraint layer,” and it recommends new test files, schema files, and CI-checkable scripts as desirable artifacts. fileciteturn6file1turn6file0

What it rejects is **open-ended generative change to product behavior** without a hard oracle. That is why it says “improve documentation quality,” “dead code elimination,” “broad refactoring,” “improve test coverage to 100%,” and “improve error handling” are bad task shapes: they are easy to satisfy in a fake way. fileciteturn5file0turn6file2turn6file3

A cleaner way to say it:

- **Allowed generative work:** generate tests, manifests, schemas, CI enforcement, contract checks, fixture-based validations, and minimal extraction/refactoring for testability where behavior must remain identical. fileciteturn6file0turn6file1
- **Disfavored generative work:** generate new runtime behavior, broad refactors, “quality improvements,” or deletions unless an existing oracle already pins behavior down. fileciteturn6file2turn6file3

The report is explicit that after the preservative layers are in place, **more generative follow-on work becomes safe**. It says that once CI, tests, and surface manifests exist, then dependency upgrades, refactors, enhancements, deeper contract tests, mutation testing, and even benchmark-driven optimization become safer. fileciteturn6file2turn6file4

So the answer is: **not “nothing generative,” but “generative only when tightly oracle-constrained.”**
From a product pov
I’m reframing it at the product layer: which task shapes create real user value versus just preserving a contract.
I have the shape now: product-wise, the report is really saying “don’t let the agent invent value unless you can measure that value externally.”

From a product POV, **no**. “Preservative only” would be too conservative.

The better reading is: **don’t ask the agent to generate product value unless the value is externally checkable.** The report is optimizing for autonomous-task safety, so it naturally ranks contract locks, CI gates, and schema tests very highly because they “lock the public contract,” “create machine-checkable contracts,” and “unblock all future CI-gated work.” fileciteturn8file0turn8file1

So product-wise, the sequence is:

1. **Protect the existing user-visible surface first.**  
   That is why API surface manifests, CLI JSON schema locks, route smoke oracles, and CI gates rank so highly. They stop fake wins like deleting features or narrowing outputs. fileciteturn8file0turn8file1

2. **Then do constrained generative work on top of that surface.**  
   The report explicitly says follow-on work becomes viable once the oracle exists: integration tests, round-trip tests, DOM rendering tests, refactoring with confidence, API schema validation, coverage-guided gap filling, mutation testing, CLI preflight harnesses, and e2e activation. That is not preservative-only; it is **generation after instrumentation**. fileciteturn8file1turn8file2

From a PM lens, the real split is not “preservative vs generative.” It is:

- **Bad product task:** “improve quality,” “reduce complexity,” “refactor,” “improve error handling.” Those are easy to Goodhart because they do not pin user value. fileciteturn8file1turn8file2
- **Good product task:** “add or complete a user-visible surface with an objective acceptance test.” Examples in the report are route smoke oracles from eval specs, OpenAPI contract tests with a committed gap report, and schema-validated CLI outputs. Those create or clarify real product surface while staying hard to game. fileciteturn7file4turn8file0

So the product answer is:

**Start preservative, but only as a staging layer. Then move into generative work that is spec-backed, fixture-backed, or contract-backed.**  
That is the anti-Goodhart version of product progress.

What’s important I’m week 14 of pregnancy. Are there food I should eat more of?

At **week 14**, you’re officially in the **second trimester** — energy often picks up here, and the baby’s growth accelerates. Key focuses now include supporting **rapid fetal development**, especially **muscle and tissue formation**, **brain development**, and **placenta growth**.

### Key Nutrients to Focus On:
Here’s what matters most now, especially considering your dietary restrictions (no dairy, nuts, seeds, or legumes):

#### 1. **Protein**
Supports baby’s tissue growth (including brain) and your expanding uterus/blood supply.
- **Good options for you:**  
  - Lean meats (chicken, turkey, beef)
  - Eggs
  - Fish (see safety note below)
  - Tofu or soy if tolerated (you’ve mentioned legumes are restricted — clarify if soy is okay)

#### 2. **Iron**
Supports increased blood volume and prevents anemia.
- **Sources you can eat:**  
  - Red meat
  - Chicken and turkey (especially thighs)
  - Iron-fortified cereals
  - Cooked spinach and kale  
  - Pair with vitamin C (like bell peppers or strawberries) to increase absorption

#### 3. **Folate (Vitamin B9)**
Still important for cell division and placenta development.
- **Sources:**  
  - Dark leafy greens  
  - Asparagus, avocado  
  - Fortified cereals

#### 4. **Calcium (since you avoid dairy)**
Still needed for baby’s bones, and your body will take from your own bones if needed.
- **Non-dairy sources:**  
  - Canned salmon/sardines with bones  
  - Calcium-fortified plant milks (if you tolerate them)  
  - Cooked bok choy, broccoli, okra

#### 5. **Omega-3s (esp. DHA)**
For baby’s brain and eye development.
- **Safe sources:**  
  - Low-mercury fish like salmon, sardines, trout (2x/week)  
  - Algae-based DHA supplements if you avoid fish

---

### General Tips
- Stay hydrated (especially as blood volume grows)
- Eat smaller meals more often if still nauseous or bloated
- Keep up prenatal vitamins with folic acid, iron, and DHA

- **Covert operations by Mossad**:  
  - Israel reportedly used a clandestine drone network inside Iran to disable missile launchers and air defenses before air strikes commenced—marking a new hybrid warfare phase combining espionage and kinetic strikes citeturn0search35.

What’s the signpost paint?

The **"signpost paint" movement** is a form of **symbolic civil disobedience** in Iran that has emerged in 2025, especially in Tehran and major cities. Protesters are **spray-painting or defacing street signs that bear the names of regime figures**, particularly those linked to repression, executions, or IRGC leadership.

---

### 🔎 What It Is:
- Protesters target **street signs named after regime loyalists**, martyrs of the Islamic Revolution, or IRGC commanders.
- They **cross out names**, **replace them with names of protesters killed in the 2022–2023 uprisings**, or spray messages like “**death to the dictator**” or “**justice for Mahsa Amini**.”
- Some signs are outright torn down or replaced with symbolic names like **“Woman, Life, Freedom Street.”**

---

### 🧠 Why It Matters:
- It’s **nonviolent**, decentralized, and hard to police.
- It reflects how deep **resistance to state propaganda** runs, even in daily life like public navigation.
- The government views it as a “counter-revolutionary” act; there have been **surveillance crackdowns** and attempts to repaint or replace signs overnight.

---

### 🧭 Cultural Impact:
This movement echoes historical precedents like:
- **Graffiti during apartheid South Africa**
- **Solidarity posters in 1980s Poland**
- **Anti-regime tagging during Arab Spring**

It's part of a broader trend where **symbolism and visibility** replace mass protest when the cost of direct action is too high.

Here are photos showing various forms of symbolic graffiti, murals, and street art—centered around the *“Woman, Life, Freedom”* (Farsi: *Žan Zendegi Āzādī*) slogan that has become central to the movement.

---

## 🎨 Signpost Paint (Feb 2025)

Recent incidents involved **“No Entry” street signs** across Tehran, Karaj, and Hamadan being **painted with a green stripe** over the usual white bar citeturn0search0.  
- **Green** symbolizes **hope, life, and resistance**, flipping the sign’s denial message toward protest and possibility.  
- The action is **anonymous and decentralized**, making it hard for authorities to remove and giving it a broad reach.  
- It aligns with earlier symbolic protests—like spray-painting street signs, walls, murals, and public art—leveraging public infrastructure as a canvas for dissent citeturn0search0turn0search3turn0search2.

---

## 🧭 Why It Matters

- **Symbolic protest**: Small yet visible acts like painting signposts signal defiance and foster unity without mass gatherings.
- **Cultural resonance**: These tactics echo broader practices—murals, graffiti, performance art—seen during the *“Woman, Life, Freedom”* protests and following Jina (Mahsa) Amini’s death in 2022 citeturn0search2turn0search3turn0search7turn0search8.
- **Difficult to suppress**: Anonymous artists and ordinary citizens can carry out such work spontaneously, making crackdowns less effective.

---

## 🖼️ Some Notable Slogans & Visuals

- **Žan, Zendegi, Āzādī** (“Woman, Life, Freedom”)—often spray‑painted on walls, highways, and public spaces as an anthem of protest citeturn0search3turn0search2turn0search7.
- **Repurposed street signs**: Some have been retitled with names of protesters killed during uprisings or renamed “Woman, Life, Freedom Street” citeturn0search3turn0search2.
- **Visual art and murals**: Public fountains were tinted red (symbolic martyrdom), and murals appeared nationwide—all forms of visual resistance by anonymous creators citeturn0search2turn0search7.

---

## 🧠 The Bigger Picture

At what point should I worry about pain during second trimester

Some discomfort in the second trimester is normal, but **certain types of pain are red flags** and should be checked out immediately. Here's a quick breakdown:

---

### ✅ **Normal (but monitor):**
- **Round ligament pain**: Sharp, quick jabs or dull aches in the lower belly or groin, especially when changing position.
- **Mild back pain**: Due to posture changes and hormone loosening of joints.
- **Mild cramping**: As the uterus expands.

---

### ❗**Call your doctor if you experience:**

#### 1. **Persistent or severe abdominal pain**
   - Especially if it’s **one-sided**, constant, or intensifies over time.
   - Could signal **placental abruption**, **preterm labor**, or **appendicitis**.

#### 2. **Pain with bleeding, fluid leakage, or cramping**
   - Could indicate **miscarriage**, **placenta previa**, or **preterm labor**.

#### 3. **Severe back pain**
   - Especially if it’s **sudden, rhythmic**, or paired with cramping—can be a sign of **preterm labor**.

#### 4. **Pain during urination**
   - Can indicate a **UTI** or **kidney infection**, which can be dangerous in pregnancy.

#### 5. **Shoulder pain**
   - Rare, but can signal **internal bleeding** (e.g., from a ruptured cyst or ectopic pregnancy if not previously caught).

What is placenta previa

**Placenta previa** is a condition where the **placenta lies low in the uterus and partially or completely covers the cervix** (the opening to the birth canal). This becomes a problem as the pregnancy progresses and especially during delivery.

---

### 🩸 Why it's serious:
- The placenta needs to move **out of the way** of the cervix for a vaginal birth.
- If it stays over or too close to the cervix, it can cause **severe bleeding**, especially in the second or third trimester and during labor.

---

### 🔍 Types:
1. **Complete previa** – placenta fully covers the cervix.
2. **Partial previa** – placenta partially covers it.
3. **Marginal or low-lying** – placenta is near the cervix but not covering it.

---

### 📆 What happens:
- It's often found on the **mid-pregnancy ultrasound** (around 18–20 weeks).
- Sometimes the placenta **"moves up"** as the uterus grows, resolving the problem by the third trimester.

---

### ⚠️ Symptoms:
- **Painless vaginal bleeding** in the second or third trimester.
- Possibly no symptoms—only caught on routine scan.

---

### 🩺 If diagnosed:
- You may need **pelvic rest** (no sex, no vaginal exams).
- Close monitoring.
- Delivery via **C-section** if the placenta hasn’t moved by term.

---

- Hormones slow digestion and growing uterus puts pressure on intestines.

- As the uterus grows, there’s general stretching of muscles and tissue.
- Usually mild but can feel like dull, ongoing cramping.

- Cramps that are:
  - **Rhythmic** or come in waves.
  - Don't go away with rest or hydration.
  - May be felt in the **back**, too.
- May be accompanied by:
  - **Pressure** in pelvis.
  - **Increased discharge**, bleeding, or fluid leakage.

#### 2. **UTI or Kidney Issue**
- Can cause **cramping or back pain**.
- Especially if paired with:
  - Burning when peeing.
  - Frequent urge to pee.
  - Fever or chills.

#### 3. **Placental Issues**
- Like **placenta abruption** (rare in second trimester).
- Usually causes **sudden, severe pain** with or without bleeding.

Is sleeping on my back ok?

Sleeping on your back in the **second trimester and beyond** is **generally *not recommended*** — but **don’t panic** if you wake up that way occasionally.

---

### 🟠 **Why it’s discouraged (around 20+ weeks):**
- When you lie flat on your back, the **heavy uterus can compress the inferior vena cava**, a major vein that returns blood to your heart.
- This can:
  - Reduce **blood flow to your baby**
  - Cause you to feel **dizzy, nauseated, or short of breath**
  - Lower your **blood pressure**

---

### ✅ **Best position:**
- **Left side** is ideal:
  - Improves blood flow to the placenta
  - Helps kidney function and reduces swelling

---

### 💡 *But real-life tip:*
- It’s **OK if you wake up on your back** — your body will usually shift before it becomes harmful.
- To help stay on your side:
  - Use a **pillow behind your back** so you can’t roll flat
  - Or **tilt slightly** with a small wedge under your right hip (this still protects blood flow)

Copper pesticide for plants harmful to use while pregnant?

Yes, **copper-based pesticides** (like copper sulfate or copper oxychloride) can pose **potential risks during pregnancy**, especially with **frequent or unprotected exposure**. While copper is a natural element and small amounts are essential in the diet, **excessive exposure to copper dust or residues** from pesticides can be harmful. Key considerations:

### Potential Risks:
- **Inhalation** of copper dust or spray mist can irritate the lungs and mucous membranes.
- **Skin contact** with concentrated forms may cause irritation or allergic reactions.
- **Systemic exposure** (especially chronic) may affect liver function or contribute to oxidative stress—something best avoided during pregnancy.

- Avoid handling or applying copper pesticides yourself.
- If it must be used:
  - Have someone else apply it.
  - Stay indoors or well away during application and until it dries completely.
  - Wear gloves and a mask if accidental handling is necessary.
  - Wash any produce thoroughly before eating.

Why does cramping get better after first trimester?

Cramping tends to improve after the first trimester of pregnancy for several reasons:

### 1. **Uterus Stabilizes in the Pelvis**
   - In early pregnancy, cramping often comes from the uterus stretching and growing. As it expands, it pulls on ligaments and structures in the pelvis.
   - Around the end of the first trimester (12–14 weeks), the uterus moves up and out of the pelvis into the abdominal cavity. This shift reduces pressure and tension on nearby structures, often relieving cramping.

### 2. **Hormonal Adjustments**
   - Early pregnancy brings a surge of hormones like **progesterone** and **relaxin**, which relax the uterus but can also affect the gastrointestinal tract and ligaments, leading to cramp-like sensations.
   - By the second trimester, hormone levels stabilize, and the body is more adapted to them, so symptoms like cramping often lessen.

### 3. **Decreased Implantation-Related Changes**
   - Cramping in the first trimester can be due to implantation and early placental development.
   - Once the placenta is fully formed (usually by week 12–14), those early developmental processes settle down, reducing associated discomfort.

### 4. **Digestive System Adjusts**
   - Progesterone slows digestion early on, causing bloating and gas, which can feel like cramps.
   - As your body adapts, some digestive symptoms ease up, making cramping less noticeable.

If cramping continues into the second trimester or becomes severe, it should be evaluated, but for many people, it naturally fades as pregnancy progresses.

Log snippet:

* **`ERR_PNPM_PREPARE_PACKAGE`**: pnpm is in its “prepare this git dependency” phase. This happens for git / tarball deps that need to be built. citeturn5view0  
* **Automatic `npm install`**: When installing a package from git, if that package has a `prepare` script, npm’s rules say its `dependencies` *and* `devDependencies` are installed and the `prepare` script is run before the package is packed/linked. pnpm follows this lifecycle behavior. citeturn1view4turn1view3turn1view5  
* **`spawn ENOENT`**: The system tried to spawn the named executable (`npm` in this case) but it was not found (ENOENT = “no such file or directory”), usually because it’s missing from `PATH` or not installed. citeturn8view0turn8view1

`script-shell` (npm) / `scriptShell` (pnpm) only selects *which shell* (`/bin/sh`, `bash.exe`, `pwsh`, etc.) is used to interpret *package scripts* (the command strings in `"scripts"`). * The failure isn’t caused by your `prepare` script at all.

## What’s really going on

| Step | What pnpm does | Why it breaks |
|------|----------------|--------------|
|1|Clones `vana‑sdk.git` into `~/.local/share/pnpm/store/v10/tmp/_tmp_…`|ordinary behaviour for any **git** dependency|
|2|Sees `"prepare": "npm run build"` in the cloned `package.json`|git packages must be built locally 📦|
|3|_Before_ running that script, pnpm tries to install the SDK’s **dev‑dependencies** by spawning → `npm install`|This always runs with the “npm‑install” lifecycle hook|
|4|pnpm **creates a minimal PATH** that only contains `./node_modules/.bin` and its own internal tools directory|Node ≥ 20 installs `npm` _outside_ those paths (Corepack), so `npm` isn’t found|
|5|`spawn ENOENT` → `ERR_PNPM_PREPARE_PACKAGE` and the whole install aborts citeturn17view0|

---

## Why your manual checks were misleading

* `npm --version` worked in your interactive shell because **nvm** had already amended `$PATH`.  
* Inside pnpm’s sandbox _that change is stripped out_, so the shim that launches Corepack’s copy of npm never appears.

(You can prove this by running `pnpm install --reporter=ndjson | jq '. | select(.name=="lifecycle")'` – you’ll see the truncated `env.PATH` that pnpm passes to the child process.)

---

## Fixes & work‑arounds

### 1. Publish a pre‑built SDK (the long‑term, correct fix)

1. **Move your build step to `prepublishOnly`** instead of `prepare`.  
   * `prepublishOnly` runs _only_ for `npm publish`, never when consumers install from npm or git docs 🗒️ citeturn27search0
2. Commit the compiled `dist/` (or whatever) and list it in `"files"` so that end‑users get ready‑to‑use code.
3. Cut a new tag and ask consumers to depend on the registry artefact (`"vana-sdk": "^1.1.0"`) instead of the git URL.

Result: no git prepare step ⇒ pnpm never needs that inner `npm install`, so the error disappears for everyone.

### 2. Quick local band‑aid (if you must keep the git dependency for now)

```bash
# Make sure Corepack has placed a real npm binary into ~/.node/corepack
corepack enable npm@latest

# Expose it to pnpm’s sandbox by creating a hard link inside pnpm’s own tool dir
ln -sf "$(which npm)" ~/.local/share/pnpm/.tools/node/bin/npm
```

*pnpm looks in that `.tools` directory first, so the link satisfies the spawn.*

> ⚠️ Do this only in CI or locally; it’s a hack, not a general‑purpose solution.

### 3. Downgrade pnpm (works because older versions inherit PATH)

```bash
npm i -g pnpm@8                     # last LTS that still copied the full PATH

```

---

## Bottom line

* The SDK isn’t “low‑quality”; the failure is a **pnpm/Node 24 interaction** that hides `npm` from lifecycle scripts.
* The sustainable fix is to **stop shipping source‑only git dependencies** and publish compiled artefacts, using `prepublishOnly` instead of `prepare`.
* Until then, either expose a real `npm` binary inside pnpm’s tool directory or stick to pnpm 8, which doesn’t strip PATH.

what is application/schema+json ? is it fundamentally a different kind of thing than what dialect represents for us?

**`application/schema+json` in one sentence**  
It’s the official IANA-registered MIME type that says “the payload you’re receiving *is itself a JSON Schema document*.” citeturn0search0

### How it’s used
| Layer | Role of `application/schema+json` | Who consumes it? |
|-------|-----------------------------------|------------------|
| HTTP / APIs | Goes in `Content-Type` or `Accept` headers so clients and servers know the body is a JSON Schema, not ordinary JSON. | Browsers, API gateways, code gens, etc. |
| File metadata | A `.json` file can be tagged with this media type to signal “open me with a JSON-Schema-aware tool.” | Editors, linters, validators |

### How that differs from *our* `dialect` field
| Our contract field | What it expresses | Example values |
|--------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| `dialect` | Which **language/format** the *embedded* schema is written in. | `"sqlite"` (DDL string) vs `"json"` (JSON Schema object) |
| (optional) `type`  | Shorthand for dialect if you want a friendlier label. | `"sql"` |

So:

* **Scope:** `application/schema+json` is a transport-level label; `dialect` is content-level metadata inside the contract.
* **Granularity:** The media type doesn’t tell you which *draft* of JSON Schema you’re using—that’s handled by the `$schema` URI inside the document (e.g. `https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema`). citeturn0search2
* **Relationship:** For any contract where `dialect: "json"`, you’d likely serve that contract (or just its `schema` section) using `Content-Type: application/schema+json`.

In short: same ecosystem, different layers—`application/schema+json` labels the *HTTP payload*, while `dialect` tells downstream code how to parse the `schema` property.

**Short answer:** You probably *don’t* want to throw away your `dialect` idea entirely, but you *can* make it line up with existing IANA media-type conventions so you get the best of both worlds.

---

### What “off-the-shelf” handles

| Need | Widely-recognised thing that covers it | Gaps you’ll hit |
|------|----------------------------------------|-----------------|
| “This document **is a schema**” | `application/schema+json` for JSON Schema docs citeturn4view0 | Only covers JSON Schema. |
| “This document **is SQL**” | `application/sql` citeturn3view0 | Doesn’t tell you which SQL flavour. |
| “This file **is a SQLite DB**” | `application/vnd.sqlite3` (or historic `application/x-sqlite3`) citeturn0search1 | Again, flavour only for SQLite; nothing for MySQL, Postgres, etc. |
| Schema versioning inside JSON Schema | `$schema` keyword inside the document citeturn2search0 | Doesn’t exist for DDL strings. |

So there **is** a standard signalling mechanism (media types) but it’s coarse-grained; it doesn’t handle vendor-specific SQL or other exotic schema languages.

---

### What to do with `dialect`

1. **Keep the field, but make its values media-type-ish.**  
   *Examples*  
   ```jsonc
   { "dialect": "application/schema+json" }
   { "dialect": "application/vnd.sqlite3" }   // or "application/sql;flavor=sqlite"
   ```
   Now any HTTP transport can copy that straight into `Content-Type`, and tooling that already understands media types will “just work”.

2. **Let the schema itself speak where it can.**  
   * If `dialect` starts with `application/schema+json`, your validators should look at the embedded `$schema` URL to pick the exact JSON Schema draft.  
   * If it’s SQL, you still need a way to say “this is Postgres 16 DDL”. You could add an optional `vendor` or `flavor` parameter (mirroring what the RFC allows for `application/sql`).

3. **Document a mapping table once and enforce it everywhere.**  
   Your meta-schema (the one we drafted) can simply restrict `dialect` to an enum of approved media-types plus any parameters you care about. That keeps producers honest and consumers predictable.

---

### Deprecate or rename?

* **Rename to `mediaType`?** Fine—people instantly recognise what it is.
* **Completely drop it?** Risky; you’d lose the ability to say “this schema is a SQLite DDL string” versus “a Postgres one” unless you invent *another* knob.

**Recommendation:** **deprecate the old free-text `dialect` values**, but keep the field (rename if you like) and back it with the IANA media-type registry. That satisfies open standards *and* your ecosystem’s stricter needs without a painful migration.
After an embryo is placed during IVF procedure what happens?

After an embryo is transferred during an IVF (in vitro fertilization) procedure, several key steps take place in hopes of a successful pregnancy:

### 1. **Embryo Transport and Attachment (Days 1–5)**
- The embryo is placed directly into the uterus using a thin catheter.
- It floats freely for a few days (usually 1–2 days).
- If it's viable, it begins to **implant** in the uterine lining (endometrium), typically around **Day 6–10 after egg retrieval** (or **1–5 days after transfer**, depending on embryo age).

### 2. **Implantation (Days 5–10 after transfer)**
- The embryo **attaches to the uterine lining** and begins to burrow in.
- It starts producing **hCG (human chorionic gonadotropin)**, the hormone detected in pregnancy tests.
- Successful implantation triggers hormonal changes to support pregnancy.

### 3. **Early Development and Hormone Support**
- If implantation occurs, the embryo becomes a **blastocyst**, then develops into a **fetus**.
- Most patients continue taking **progesterone** and sometimes **estrogen** to support the uterine lining and prevent early miscarriage.
  
### 4. **Pregnancy Test (~10–14 days after transfer)**
- A **beta hCG blood test** is done to confirm pregnancy.
- If positive, hormone levels are monitored to ensure they rise appropriately.
- If negative, the cycle is considered unsuccessful, and your clinic will discuss next steps.

If it’s not successful what happens?

If the embryo transfer is **not successful**, it means the embryo did **not implant** in the uterine lining, or implantation began but didn’t progress. Here’s what typically happens:

### 1. **No Implantation**
- The embryo may simply not attach to the endometrium.
- The body doesn’t produce significant levels of **hCG**, so a **pregnancy test will be negative**.
- Eventually, the embryo is reabsorbed or shed by the body.

### 2. **Biochemical Pregnancy (Early Loss)**
- Sometimes implantation starts, and hCG is detectable, but the pregnancy doesn’t progress.
- This is called a **biochemical pregnancy**, and the hCG levels will rise slightly but then drop.
- You may get a faint positive pregnancy test, followed by a period.

### 3. **Menstruation**
- If the embryo doesn’t implant or fails early, you will typically **get your period within 9–14 days after the transfer**.
- It might be a bit earlier or later than usual and can be heavier, lighter, or more crampy depending on hormones used during the cycle.

### 4. **Stopping Medications**
- If the pregnancy test is negative, your clinic will tell you when to **stop progesterone and other medications**.
- Once those are stopped, a withdrawal bleed (period) usually follows within a few days.

### 5. **Emotional and Physical Recovery**
- A failed transfer can be emotionally hard. Many clinics offer counseling or support resources.
- Physically, recovery is usually quick; your cycle will return to normal within a few weeks.

Does it feel like a miscarriage?

A failed embryo transfer can sometimes **feel like a miscarriage**, but it depends on how far things progressed:

---

### 🔹 If the embryo **never implanted**:
- You’ll just get your period, possibly a bit later than usual.
- **Physically**, it might feel like a normal or slightly heavier period.
- **Emotionally**, though, it can be just as devastating as a miscarriage—especially because of the hopes pinned on the cycle.

---

### 🔹 If there was a **biochemical pregnancy** (early implantation, then failure):
- You may get a **faint positive test**, followed by bleeding.
- This is an **early miscarriage**, but it happens so soon (usually before 5 weeks) that it often feels like a period.
- Some people feel cramps, back pain, or emotional distress similar to a very early loss.

---

### 🔹 If implantation progressed more (e.g. a gestational sac forms) but fails:
- That’s a **clinical miscarriage**—less common in IVF at this stage, but more intense physically and emotionally.
- Bleeding and cramping are more noticeable, and you may need medical management depending on how far along it was.

---

In short:  
- **Physically**, most failed transfers do *not* feel like later miscarriages—they’re often just delayed or irregular periods.  
- **Emotionally**, it *can absolutely* feel like a miscarriage, because you’ve already been through so much to get to this point.

Bowen Theory, developed by psychiatrist Murray Bowen, views schizophrenia not as an isolated individual illness but as a symptom of extreme emotional dysfunction within the family system. According to Bowen, schizophrenia arises from intense emotional fusion—where family members are overly emotionally dependent on each other—combined with chronic anxiety passed down across generations. The person who becomes schizophrenic is typically the most emotionally vulnerable member of the family, often serving as the "identified patient" who absorbs and expresses the family's unresolved tensions.

Does Bowen Theory say schizophrenia is not biological?

Bowen Theory does **not deny** that schizophrenia has biological aspects, but it emphasizes that **biology alone is not sufficient to explain the disorder**. Murray Bowen believed that while genetic and neurological factors play a role, the development of schizophrenia is deeply influenced by the **emotional processes in the family system**, especially patterns of intense emotional fusion and multigenerational transmission of anxiety.

In short, Bowen saw schizophrenia as a **biopsychosocial phenomenon**—where biology may create a predisposition, but family dynamics and emotional processes strongly shape how (or whether) that vulnerability manifests.

- **Systemic Insight**: Professionals appreciate that Bowen Theory brought attention to the **family as a unit** rather than focusing only on the individual.
- **Therapeutic Value**: Some therapists, especially in **family therapy or systems therapy**, still use aspects of Bowen Theory to address emotional patterns and anxiety transmission in families.

### 2. **Criticism and Limitations:**
- **Biological Advances**: Neuroscience and genetics have shown strong **biological underpinnings** for schizophrenia (e.g., dopamine dysregulation, heritability), so most professionals view the condition as primarily **biological with psychosocial modifiers**, not the reverse.
- **Blame Concerns**: Critics argue that early family systems models (including Bowen's) could be interpreted as **blaming families**, especially parents, for causing schizophrenia—an idea now widely rejected.
- **Empirical Support**: Bowen Theory lacks **rigorous empirical validation**, especially compared to cognitive-behavioral or pharmacological models used today.

### 3. **Current Status:**
- Bowen’s ideas are mostly used in **family systems therapy contexts**, not in mainstream psychiatry or clinical psychology for treating schizophrenia. Today’s dominant model is the **biopsychosocial model**, where:
  - **Biological**: Genes, brain chemistry
  - **Psychological**: Coping skills, trauma
  - **Social**: Family, culture, stressors

In sum, Bowen’s contribution is seen as insightful from a systems and family dynamic standpoint, but **insufficient** as a standalone explanation or treatment model for schizophrenia.

What would you say to a family systems therapist who suggests to her patient that their child is much less likely to develop schizophrenia because of healthy family dynamics?

Here’s the kind of balanced, evidence-based reply you could offer—one that validates the value of healthy family functioning while keeping the science straight and avoiding false reassurance:

---

**“Healthy family dynamics are absolutely protective factors, but they don’t remove biological vulnerability.”**

1. **Start by affirming the good news.**  
   *“Strong communication, low chronic stress, and emotional support all lower general mental-health risk, and they’re associated with better outcomes for people who already have schizophrenia.”*

2. **Add the biological context.**  
   *“Schizophrenia remains highly heritable—about a 7–10 % lifetime risk for a child with one affected parent versus roughly 1 % in the general population. Genes and neurodevelopment set the basic level of vulnerability; environment modifies how (or whether) that vulnerability shows up.”*

3. **Frame family health as a **protective—not preventive—factor.**  
   *“Think of family climate like cholesterol management in someone with a genetic risk for heart disease: helpful, sometimes dramatically so, but not a complete guarantee.”*

4. **Highlight the diathesis-stress model the field now embraces.**  
   *“The consensus model says genes (the diathesis) + stressors (including—but not limited to—family stress) interact. Lowering one side of the equation matters, yet the diathesis is still there.”*

5. **Encourage ongoing monitoring, not complacency.**  
   *“Because onset typically occurs in late teens to early adulthood, it pays to stay alert to early warning signs—social withdrawal, decline in functioning, unusual perceptual experiences—regardless of how healthy the family system is.”*

6. **Cite evidence-based buffers beyond family climate.**  
   - **Perinatal health and childhood nutrition**  
   - **Trauma prevention**  
   - **Substance-use avoidance (especially cannabis before age 25)**  
   - **Prompt treatment of prodromal symptoms**  

7. **Gently correct any suggestion of parental ‘control’ or blame.**  
   *“Parents can influence the stress environment, but they don’t ‘cause’ or ‘cure’ schizophrenia. Let’s keep the focus on factors we can realistically modify while acknowledging the limits of our influence.”*

8. **Offer collaborative next steps.**  
   *“We can work together on maintaining the family strengths you already have, and we can create a plan for early detection and rapid intervention just in case—much the way families with heart-disease genes keep an eye on blood pressure.”*

---

**Bottom line:**  
Healthy family dynamics meaningfully reduce stress and improve resilience, but they should be presented as **risk-reducing**, not **risk-eliminating**. Anchoring the conversation in the biopsychosocial evidence base protects families from misplaced guilt **and** from a false sense of security.
How risk reducing?

**Quantitatively, “healthy family dynamics” can cut risk somewhere between one-half and four-fifths, depending on which outcome you look at and which study you cite.** Here are the two best-documented examples: | Outcome studied | “Stressed / dysfunctional” family group | “Healthy / low-stress” family group | Approx. relative-risk reduction | |-----------------|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------|---------------------------------| | **First-episode incidence in high-genetic-risk adoptees**
*(Finnish Adoption Study, Tienari et al., 1994 → 2004)* | 36-38 % developed a schizophrenia-spectrum disorder by early adulthood | 5-7 % developed a disorder (≈ general-population base rate) | **≈ 80 % lower risk** | | **Relapse after an index psychotic episode**
*(Expressed Emotion meta-analyses; Vaughn & Leff 1976 → Butzlaff & Hooley 1998)* | 45-55 % relapse within 9-12 mo in **high-EE** families | 15-25 % relapse in **low-EE** families | **≈ 60 % lower risk** | ### How to read those numbers * **They’re conditional.** The Finnish data apply only to children who already carry a strong genetic loading (biological parent with schizophrenia). The EE numbers apply to people who have **already had** a psychotic episode. * **They’re population averages, not guarantees.** A supportive family climate shifts probability; it doesn’t render the diathesis inert. * **“Healthy” ≠ perfect.** In the Finnish study, researchers defined a “healthy” family as one free of severe discord, boundary confusion, or chronic criticism—not a utopia. ### Practical takeaway - **Best-case scenario:** In a high-risk child, maintaining low chronic stress, low criticism, and good problem-solving communication might bring lifetime risk down from roughly **1-in-3 to about 1-in-20**—still above zero, but close to the population baseline. - **For relapse prevention:** Family psycho-education aimed at reducing expressed emotion reliably **halves** the chance of rehospitalization over the following year. So the reduction is *meaningful*—often on the order of **50-80 %**—but never absolute. Healthy family dynamics should be framed as a powerful **risk-modifier**, not an iron-clad safeguard.

- Tend to be **reclusive** and not aggressive, but should never be handled

#### 2. **Prefabricated or Modular Guest Houses**
- **Base Models**: Starting around $13,500 for basic 80 sq ft units
- **Mid-Range Options**: $23,000–$70,000 for 160–240 sq ft units with insulation and basic amenities citeturn0search6
- **Fully Outfitted Units**: Up to $100,000 or more, depending on customization and finishes
- **Additional Costs**: Foundation, utilities, permits, and site work can add $10,000–$30,000

- **Enhanced Conversions**: $50,000–$100,000 if adding plumbing, insulation, and HVAC
- **Advantages**: Utilizes existing structures, reducing construction time and costs

#### 4. **DIY or Minimalist Builds**
- **Cost Range**: Under $30,000 for simple structures under 200 sq ft without plumbing citeturn0search2
- **Considerations**: Ideal for home offices or studios; may not meet requirements for full-time habitation

---

### 🏗️ Key Factors Influencing Costs

- **Size & Design Complexity**: Larger and more complex designs increase costs.
- **Construction Method**: Custom builds are generally more expensive than prefab or conversions.
- **Finishes & Amenities**: High-end materials and full kitchens/bathrooms add to the budget.
- **Site Conditions**: Sloped lots, tree removal, or utility access can affect costs.
